tip: If someone calls claiming to be from the government and demands immediate payment, hang up. Real government agencies send letters first and never demand gift cards or wire transfers.
Never give out your Social Security number, bank account, or credit card number to someone who calls you. Call the company back using a number you trust.
If you get a text saying your package couldn't be delivered and asks you to click a link, don't tap it. Go directly to the delivery company's website instead.
Banks will never text you asking for your password or PIN. If you get a suspicious text from your bank, call them using the number on your bank card.
Before clicking any link in an email, hover over it to see where it really goes. Scammers make links look official but they lead to fake websites.
Watch for emails that create urgency like 'Your account will be closed in 24 hours!' Real companies give you time and don't threaten you.
If someone asks you to pay with gift cards, it's always a scam. No real company or government agency accepts gift cards as payment.
Scammers often pretend to be grandchildren in trouble. If you get a call like this, hang up and call your grandchild directly at their real number.
If your caller ID shows a familiar name or number, it might still be a scam. Scammers can fake caller ID to look like anyone.
Never let someone rush you into a decision about money. Tell them you need to think about it, then talk to someone you trust.
If a website asks for your personal information and the web address doesn't start with 'https://' (with the 's'), don't enter anything.
Be careful with pop-ups that say your computer has a virus. Real antivirus software doesn't ask you to call a phone number.
If someone calls saying they're from tech support and asks to remotely access your computer, hang up. Real tech companies don't cold-call customers.
If an offer sounds too good to be true, it probably is. Free vacations, lottery winnings you didn't enter, and guaranteed investment returns are almost always scams.
Scammers sometimes pretend to have the wrong number to start a friendly conversation. If a stranger texts you, don't engage.
Check the sender's email address carefully. Scammers use addresses like support@amaz0n-help.com that look real but aren't.
Keep your phone and apps updated. Updates often fix security problems that scammers try to exploit.
Use different passwords for different accounts. If one gets stolen, the others stay safe. A password manager can help.
If someone pressures you to stay on the phone and not tell anyone what's happening, that's a major warning sign of a scam.
Before buying from a new online store, search for reviews. Scam stores often have no reviews or only fake five-star ratings.
Scammers target people who recently lost a loved one. Be extra cautious about unexpected calls or emails after a loss in your family.
If someone says they're a police officer or FBI agent and demands money over the phone, it's a scam. Real law enforcement doesn't work that way.
A text from your 'bank' with a link is almost always a scam. Your real bank already has your information and won't ask for it via text.
Be suspicious of emails with spelling mistakes and poor grammar. Major companies have professional editors — scammers usually don't.
If you're asked to keep a financial transaction secret from your family, that's a red flag. Scammers isolate their victims.
Don't click on ads promising miracle cures, free money, or extreme discounts. These often lead to scam websites.
Some scammers play a recording that sounds like someone saying 'Can you hear me?' They want you to say 'yes' to record your voice for fraud.
Romance scammers build trust over weeks before asking for money. If an online friend you've never met asks for cash, it's a scam.
If you get a message saying you won a prize for a contest you didn't enter, ignore it. You can't win a contest you never entered.
Never open email attachments from unknown senders. They can contain viruses that steal your personal information.
If someone asks you to wire money to a foreign country, it's almost certainly a scam. Wire transfers can't be reversed.
Scammers may call pretending to be your utility company, threatening to shut off your power. Call your utility company directly to check.
Be careful sharing personal details on social media. Scammers use this information to make their scams more convincing.
If a charity calls asking for a donation, ask for their official website and research them first. Many fake charities exist.
If you get a text saying there's suspicious activity on your account with a link to 'verify', don't click. Call your bank directly instead.
Scammers may leave voicemails saying you owe money to the IRS. The IRS contacts people by mail first, never by threatening phone calls.
Before making any large financial decision based on a call or email, sleep on it. Scammers create fake urgency to prevent you from thinking clearly.
If an email says you need to update your payment information for a subscription, go to the company's website directly instead of clicking the email link.
Use two-factor authentication (2FA) whenever possible. It adds an extra layer of security beyond just a password.
If someone offers to help you recover money from a previous scam, be very careful. Recovery scams target people who've already been victimized.
Don't return calls to numbers you don't recognize, especially international numbers. Some scammers use premium-rate numbers that charge you.
QR codes in unexpected messages or on stickers placed over real ones can lead to scam websites. Be cautious about scanning random QR codes.
Your bank will never ask you to move money to a 'safe account.' There is no such thing. This is always a scam.
Scammers sometimes send fake invoices for things you didn't buy. Don't call the number in the email — look up the real company number instead.
If someone claims to be a friend or family member but their phone number is different, verify by calling them at a number you already have.
Free WiFi at cafes and airports can be used by hackers. Avoid doing banking or shopping on public WiFi networks.
If a caller asks you to download an app to fix a problem, refuse. Scammers use remote access apps to steal your information.
Cryptocurrency and investment scams often promise huge guaranteed returns. No legitimate investment is guaranteed to make money.
Scam texts often come from short codes or unusual numbers. Your bank typically messages from a consistent, recognizable number.
If you think you've been scammed, act fast. Contact your bank immediately, change your passwords, and report it to the authorities.
